file_name: Ayas - 2022 - Multiclass skin lesion classification in dermoscopic images using swin transformer model.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Swin Transformer model, which integrates CNNs and transformers, for multiclass skin lesion classification in dermoscopic images. The method was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset to classify skin lesions into multiple diagnostic categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a new swin transformer-based classification approach for multiclass skin lesion classification. It details original experimental methods, including the use of the ISIC 2019 dataset, ablation studies, and performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, balanced accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multiclass skin lesion classification in dermoscopic images using swin transformer model
brief_summary: This study proposes a Swin Transformer-based model for multiclass skin lesion classification in dermoscopic images to address challenges such as intraclass variation and class imbalance. The method integrates CNNs and transformers for end-to-end mapping without requiring prior knowledge or preprocessing stages. To handle class imbalance, the authors employed a weighted cross-entropy loss function during training. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains images from eight diagnostic categories. The proposed approach achieved a sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and balanced accuracy of 82.3%, 97.9%, 97.2%, and 82.3%, respectively. Experimental results demonstrate that the method outperforms most state-of-the-art techniques in multiclass skin lesion classification.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin lesion diagnostic categories (Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic nevus, Melanoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'multiclass skin lesion classification' using a Swin Transformer model. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic labels for dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains eight distinct classes. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and balanced accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection Challenge dataset)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing images from eight diagnostic categories. The ground truth labels for the test data were not public, so the authors split the available training data into training, validation, and test sets.

use.training: The dataset was split into training (70%), validation (10%), and testing (20%) sets. The training set was used to fine-tune the Swin Transformer model pre-trained on ImageNet.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process with a 10% split of the original training data.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, balanced accuracy) with a 20% split of the original training data.
use.other: The model was pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset before being fine-tuned on ISIC 2019.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate evaluation metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, Balanced Accuracy) and perform ablation studies, stratified by the frequency of diagnosis class.
To address class imbalance during model training, with performance compared against standard cross-entropy loss.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, Balanced Accuracy) and ablation studies without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.split: Test set (20% of original training data)
ISIC 2019.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest balanced accuracy (82.3%) among compared state-of-the-art methods. The lowest sensitivity was observed for the DF class (74.9%), and the highest for the NV class (94.9%). The weighted cross-entropy loss improved SE by 2.9% compared to standard cross-entropy loss. The Large model pretrained on ImageNet-22K yielded the best ablation results (SE 85.1%, BACC 85.1%), but the final reported results for the proposed method in the abstract and comparison tables correspond to the configuration achieving SE 82.3%, SP 97.9%, ACC 97.2%, and BACC 82.3%.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet-22K dataset (specifically for the Large variant); ImageNet-1K used for Tiny, Small, and Base variants.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (images resized to this resolution); Input feature dimension after patch partition is 48 (4x4x3).

training_details.loss_function: Weighted Cross Entropy Loss (using inverse class frequency weighting)
training_details.data_augmentation: Full data augmentation including geometric transformations (horizontal/vertical flipping) and color jittering (brightness changes)

classification_or_output_head.type: Linear classifier (implied by standard Swin Transformer architecture for classification tasks)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Swin Transformer (Tiny, Small, Base, Large variants)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization using mean and standard deviation of ImageNet pretrained model
Data augmentation: Geometric transformations (random horizontal and vertical flips)
Data augmentation: Color jitter transformations (brightness, contrast, and saturation changes)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random horizontal and vertical flips applied to images during training.
Random changes to brightness, contrast, and saturation of images during training.
Normalization using the mean and standard deviation of the ImageNet pretrained model.
All images resized to a resolution of 224x224 pixels.

conclusions: The proposed Swin Transformer model for multiclass skin lesion classification achieved a sensitivity of 82.3%, specificity of 97.9%, accuracy of 97.2%, and balanced accuracy of 82.3% on the ISIC 2019 dataset.
The proposed method demonstrated the highest balanced accuracy among compared state-of-the-art techniques for multiclass skin lesion classification.
The method leverages both transformer and CNN architectures for end-to-end mapping without requiring prior knowledge or preprocessing stages.
Class imbalance was effectively addressed using a weighted cross-entropy loss function, which improved sensitivity by 2.9% compared to standard cross-entropy loss.

The Swin Transformer Large model pretrained on the ImageNet-22K dataset yielded the best performance in ablation studies (SE 85.1%, BACC 85.1%), indicating that larger pretraining datasets improve performance for complex models.
Increasing model complexity beyond the Base variant on ImageNet-1K led to performance degradation due to insufficient training data for the more complicated model.
Full data augmentation (geometric transformations and color jittering) improved classification performance, with geometric transformations alone providing significant gains in sensitivity.
The AdamW optimizer significantly improved sensitivity (to 79.1% in ablation studies) compared to Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with momentum.

The lowest sensitivity was observed for the Dermatofibroma (DF) class (74.9%), while the highest sensitivity was for the Melanocytic Nevus (NV) class (94.9%), highlighting challenges with specific lesion types.
The study addresses challenges such as intraclass variation, interclass visual similarity, low contrast, and artifacts inherent in dermoscopic images.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (ISIC 2019) with no external validation, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic Nevus (NV) containing over 12,000 images while Dermatofibroma (DF) has only 239, potentially biasing the model towards majority classes.

The authors split the original training data into training, validation, and test sets because ground truth labels for the official test set were not public, which may introduce data leakage or selection bias compared to standard challenge protocols.

Performance varies significantly across classes; the lowest sensitivity was observed for Dermatofibroma (74.9%) and Actinic Keratosis (70.5%), indicating potential clinical risks for these specific diagnoses.
The model achieves high specificity (97.9%) but moderate sensitivity (82.3%), suggesting a tendency to minimize false positives at the cost of missing some positive cases.
The study does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, relying instead on descriptive metrics.
The model was trained on a single GPU (Nvidia GeForce GTX 1070Ti), which may limit reproducibility for researchers with different hardware constraints.

No preprocessing or segmentation steps were employed, relying entirely on end-to-end learning, which simplifies deployment but may be sensitive to image artifacts like hairs or rulers present in dermoscopic images.
